title: Modular Curves X1(n) as Moduli of Point Arrangements
speaker: Lev Borisov
abstract:
For a complex elliptic curve E and a point p of order n on it, the images of the points pk=kp under the Weierstrass embedding of E into CP2 are collinear if and only if the sum of indices is divisible by n. We prove that for n at least 10 a collection of n points in CP2  with these properties comes (generically) from a point of order n on an elliptic curve. In the process, we discover amusing identities between logarithmic derivatives of the theta function at rational points. I will also discuss potential applications of these results to bounds on the numbers of Hecke eigenforms for Γ1(n) of positive analytic rank, although this is rather speculative. 
This is joint work with Xavier Roulleau, see  https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.04364.

proof_stages.label: Weierstrass embedding and collinearity group law
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls that E=C/(Z+Z tau) embeds in CP^2 as a smooth plane cubic via the Weierstrass function, and that three points on E sum to zero exactly when their images are collinear.

proof_stages.label: Cyclic torsion matroid and realization problem
proof_stages.summary: Taking the order-n point 1/n gives n points p_0,...,p_{n-1} whose collinear triples are precisely those with i+j+k=0 mod n, motivating the reverse problem of realizing this matroid in CP^2 and leading from numerical evidence to the conjecture that the space is X_1(n).

proof_stages.label: PGL normalization and determinantal realization equations
proof_stages.summary: For n sufficiently large, four points are put in standard projective position, the remaining points are written as (1,a_k,b_k) except for the forced special point, and the required collinearities become determinant equations defining an affine realization scheme and its projective compactification.

proof_stages.label: Reconstruction of the cubic curve from a realization
proof_stages.summary: Using local coordinates s and t determined by nearby points, one computes enough points to force a unique cubic; the smooth case recovers an elliptic curve with an order-n point, while the singular case is controlled by the nodal rational curve and accounts for cuspidal boundary behavior.

proof_stages.label: Determinant-ratio formulas as modular functions
proof_stages.summary: After standardizing the elliptic-curve points by a unique PGL_3 transformation, the coordinates a_k and b_k are computed as invariant ratios of determinants, then re-expressed through elliptic functions, theta functions, and the weight-one forms S_a.

proof_stages.label: Weight-one modular-form embedding via identities among S_a
proof_stages.summary: Unexpected identities among the S_a rewrite the normalized-coordinate formulas with a common denominator, showing that the compactified realization curve is embedded by weight-one modular forms and giving the scheme-theoretic/smooth closure identification with X_1(n).

proof_stages.label: Consequences at cusps and in the arithmetic of S_a
proof_stages.summary: The S_a identities suggest modular-unit and cuspidal-Picard-group questions, describe special limiting arrangements at cusps, and motivate further arithmetic questions about spans of products of the S_a and their relation to modular forms of analytic rank zero.

examples.name: Numerical realization spaces up to n=42
examples.note: Xavier Roulleau's computed cases of the point-configuration realization space, later mentioned up to n=42, are presented as the experimental source of the conjectural identification with X_1(n).
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explains the numerical setup by normalizing four points, writing the remaining coordinates, imposing determinant collinearity equations, compactifying, and reading degrees/Hilbert data

examples.name: Small-n degeneracies such as n=2 and n=4
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker uses very small values of n to illustrate why the unmodified matroid realization problem has extra or wrong-dimensional degenerate cases and hence why the clean theorem is stated for larger n.
examples.engagement_reason: small cases such as n=2 and n=4 are only cited as degenerate, with remarks like collinearity being irrelevant or the dimension being wrong

passes.reason: For n=2 or n=4 the incidence conditions are vacuous or wrong-dimensional, showing the naive X_1(n) statement fails at small n.

examples.name: Conic-plus-line cusp arrangement for n=2m
examples.note: At certain cusps for even n, the limiting point configuration consists of specified points on a conic and a line, serving as a concrete picture of what the compactified realization space adds at the boundary.
examples.engagement_reason: the conic-plus-line cusp arrangement for n=2m is briefly described as appearing at certain cusps but not derived or analyzed

examples.name: Classical X_0(n) equations from j(tau) and j(n tau)
